transcript: 7:21. Today on the "Consult the Expert" segment, we'll be discussing a topic that's all the rage right now in the business sector. Investments in Paraguay.  As we have shown in recent days, many Brazilian companies are relocating or opening units in the neighboring country, mainly to lower tax and production costs.  And to talk about this subject, I have two experts with me today.  Emanuel Horta, specialist in ventures, investment, and entrepreneurship in Paraguay, and Cléber Gois, tax specialist.  Good morning, thank you for being here, Manuel Cléber, how are you ?  We're going to help, and it's a topic that has generated a lot of questions and curiosity.  The Brazil-European Union treaty was signed in Paraguay. Paraguay is becoming increasingly prominent. And let's get started, right? This also applies to those who recently opened a business in Paraguay. How long have you been in Paraguay? We've been together for a year and a half.  Now, a year and a half.  It's not even recent anymore, it's been a while. Our second company is newer now.  It has been open for three months.  We have two trips in Paraguay for the last one.  And today you assist entrepreneurs who want to set up shop there. Exactly.  We handle all aspects of legal and documentation support on the page. So, anyone who wants to legally enter Paraguay and get off to a good start can count on us. What are the advantages, Emanuel, of expanding the business to our neighboring country? Oh, let's think about the tax issue.  Brazil, this tax aberration we have here, simplified, 10% tax on net profit, only that for any company.  Now, we also have the issue of operating costs.  You have a cost of 16.5% of payroll for operational expenses alone.  This is also a major difference compared to Brazil.  You'll also have an energy cost that's 10 times cheaper than energy in Paraguay.  It's also frightening, isn't it? For industries that consume a lot of energy in Brazil, it's a paradise for them there.  And you'll also have simplified and inexpensive access to the international market , because companies there often get exemptions when importing.  So she imports raw materials at no cost, only paying for shipping and the cost of the merchandise, something that is also a real pain in Brazil. But he says, "Ah, all of this is very good, is there any way to improve it?"  He has.  There is a maquila regime where you pay only 1% tax on the added value.  Therefore, industries that export practically 90% or more of their production can also qualify for this regime and take advantage of Paraguay's benefits by paying virtually zero tax.   Those of you who are there, have you noticed that any particular segment is establishing itself there, like agribusiness or some other type of company, a specific sector, or is this change general? The textile sector is the most prevalent today , but it's not the best, let's say; it's not the only sector that will benefit.  Each scenario will have its own specific characteristics, and certainly the factories stand out as a whole.  Assembly, parts, text, any type of maquiladora, right, factory production, will have benefits because of these much lower costs, but also a lower tax cost.  However, it obviously needs to be adapted to fit perfectly, because most of our clients are Brazilian.  So, when he goes there, he plans to sell to Brazil as well.  So, on this trip to Brazil, we would need a tax specialist in Brazil to sort out this mess we have here, to make sense of this transition.   That's where we come in.  Cléber, good morning again.  Thank you for coming. Thanks. How to get organized, because it's not just a matter of showing up and opening a unit in Paraguay.  Sometimes it might not be worth it .  So, how can you organize yourself to take advantage of these benefits?  Fernando, we're thinking in the following way.  Before you look primarily at Paraguay, I think we could look at what's right in our own country first.  The entrepreneur needs to be very aware of all their costs, which products offer the highest profit margin, the cost of energy—they need to have that very well mapped out , because when you have that mapped out , you have clarity, right?  And with clarity, we can run simulations.  So we can take a product here in Brazil and simulate what the tax burden would be like in Paraguay.  It's worth it, there's the logistics, because before he was sometimes in Mato Grosso, now he can be in Paraguay, there will be that cost.  So , once you have this clear information, you can run simulations.  In simulations, we can create various scenarios.  Oh, is it worth going to the whole company?  Is it worthwhile for only some units to do this triangulation, where can I get the highest profit margin, taking advantage of the benefits that Manoel mentioned in Paraguay?  So, it's through simulations, because if we work based on guesswork, as I say, it's more or less like transferring the problem of addressing the problem.  In other words, I have my problem here in Brazil with high costs, but I don't know where my operation really is.  I'm only taking it to Paraguay hoping for a price reduction, which may not materialize.  That's why we say, before you think about expanding in Paraguay, understand your numbers first.  This is our recommendation as experts.  She provides consulting services, right ?  Have you ever had a situation where a client came in and said, "Hey, what about Paraguay?" and you said no? Look, in your case, I don't think it's a good idea.   Yes yes .  And recently, we had a client who mentioned a structure to us, and since we already do monthly follow-ups with him, we said: "Look, before we think about this new plan, first you need to sort things out internally. Then we can think about an expansion and growth plan, because Paraguay can be viewed in two ways: either a complete relocation of operations there, where you think about moving your company, or, as Manuel said, towards the global market, where instead of focusing on your domestic market, you can think about exporting, because going through Paraguay is much more advantageous, or you can use it as a growth arm for your operation." I think we can look at these two perspectives as local businesses. And bringing up some data we've gathered, this Malala project that Emanuel brought up isn't a recent project; it's been around for 20 years . Today there are approximately 200 companies operating within the MAC. And of those 200 companies, 70% are... Brazilian companies that sell to our domestic market, our internal market. So, people are looking at Paraguay as an extension of Brazilian operations. Yes, we even showed examples here, as you said, sometimes not all production goes to Paraguay. We showed in reports companies that go there to have part of the product manufactured there to take advantage of the import advantages, the tax advantages there, as well as in Brazil, the tax authorities will always find the best scenario, right? Because some parts of production done in Brazil give you some exemptions. So they also devise a good strategy to improve the cost and the tax they will receive in the end. Now, it's a fact that Paraguay is attractive, it's very attractive. For those watching us, especially the entrepreneur, it's worthwhile, right, when there is this tax approval, both for small and large companies, can the small ones also go? Absolutely. As I said initially, you will have a simplified tax regime . So, regardless of the scenario, with or without maquila, you already start well with only 10% tax on net profit. If you don't have profit,  It doesn't pay taxes, it's much more honest, let's say, right? Because, you might make money, but you don't have results and you'll pay taxes on your revenue. That 's extremely unfair, isn't it? But in practice, in Paraguay, you're very well regarded in that sense. Make a profit, trade, bring money into the country and we'll just take a small piece. And when someone wants to get into maquila, but does n't have such a large structure, they just need to hire four employees to have a maquila. So, they have an operation, it doesn't need to be that robust. Many people think: "Wow, only Lupo, only Estrela, only those big corporations can take advantage of this." Actually, no. You can organize yourself, create a well-structured business plan, take a small operation to Paraguay, respecting the main rules of maquila, which is to import foreign products tax-free, the vast majority of them, manufacture them within Paraguay and export them abroad, regardless of the country you're sending them from. Help us translate maquila. Maquila is a...  To produce, maquila, add value. So, in Spain, in Paraguay, there's this maquila rule. To qualify for this 1% tax regime on added value, paying almost no tax, you need to meet these pillars: import, produce domestically, add real value, have substance within the business, and export the vast majority of your production. By meeting these criteria, you can succeed. Remember that maquila must be approved; it's not something you just meet the requirements for and assume you already have it. The government needs to verify it. We take this to our new clients who are coming now; we create a plan to send to the government's commercial sector for approval. Once approved, it takes about 30 to 60 days, but as soon as it's approved, the person can move in and start operations. Of course, everything must be legal. If the person only sent the plan and doesn't yet have personal documentation or hasn't established the initial company, then it will take a little longer to organize the legal basis before starting operations. Perfect. And for guidance then... Tax practice, right, organizational, documentation, what would that be then, Cléber? Before taking that step, first, what I mentioned before, I think we can separate it into two worlds, right? The tangible, which is where we start to do simulations, to look. We can look at historical data. Today there's a percentage model there that was made available by the Industry and Commerce sector showing how many companies are operating there. So, through these indicators, we can probably model which ones have the most advantages. But then we're talking about tangible costs, right? But we can look at it at a business level, the intangible costs. What would these intangible costs be? As Manuel said, for a business owner to have a lower tax cost on payroll, they have more competitiveness. What would that be? Well, I can pay my employee better. And if you pay your employee better, wouldn't they produce more for you? So we work with that as an intangible cost. If you bring a better quality of life to a  "Employee, because today the cost of an employee for a company is one of the heaviest expenses, right? So, if you have lower costs, you can transfer some of that value to your employee, therefore they will have a better quality of life, they will produce more. So, in addition to the tangible cost of reduced taxes, lower operating costs, and electricity, you can also think about the intangible cost of an employee producing more. So, you can evaluate all these aspects. Now, to conclude, Cléber, moving from the technical to the more opinion-based part, do you believe that Brazil is driving entrepreneurs away with all this tax burden we have? Look, if we look at the movement that's going there, the company is taking, for me, every entrepreneur who moves an operation to Paraguay, is giving a response to the Brazilian government. This operation is not advantageous for me. I 'm taking my investment to Paraguay. This is not healthy for Brazil. Brazil has to produce wealth. So, our..."  My opinion is that this movement isn't right in Brazil and it's likely to get worse with the tax reform. You yourself aren't going back to that, my friend, certainly not. I'm hearing a lot of business owners say, "Man, I pay three times more to the government than what I receive, than what I take out of my own pocket." The government is the most greedy partner in the company, and now it's making the situation worse, it's now taking away the tax beforehand to disrupt cash flow, it's increasing the base rate. So, it was becoming unviable, because it was already unviable. No serious company can operate with its cash flow being drained all the time. And Brazil has always worked like this, that's why there weren't taxes on dividends, because it already took a lot from the business owner internally. Nobody has this tax system like Brazil in the whole world. So, you take the money from the company, you take it back when there's a profit, when the guy is going to buy an yacht, is going to buy a mansion, okay, let's take a piece. It's a pleasure, you two have a great time here. Cléber, always a pleasure, have a great Thursday and I'm sure we'll talk a lot more about this.

Sejam muito bem-vindos a Meu País Paraguai. Meu nome é Manuel Horta. Para quem não me conhece, eu sou sócio fundador da empresa e hoje nós vamos falar um pouco mais sobre as vantagens de ter uma empresa no Paraguai. Bem, todo mundo sabe que o Paraguai tá em alta, todo mundo tá falando sobre isso. Na verdade, até alguns grandes influenciadores que eu não imaginei em falar estão falando, quer dizer que furou a bolha, tá muito viral esse assunto, mas não é por causa do Paraguai, não, é por causa do Brasil que tá derretendo. As coisas aqui estão bem tristes, é complicado, mas infelizmente ou felizmente nós temos o lenço para quem tá chorando. Piados à parte é o seguinte: o Paraguai ele sempre foi um refúgio para empresas prosperarem. A lei de maquilla, por exemplo, ela é dos anos 90, então não é uma coisa nova, não é uma novidade. O sistema tributário paraguaio também era simplificado há muito tempo atrás, porém agora ele foi atualizado para simplificar como a gente entende o sistema, mas não o sistema em si, porque ele já era simplificado. Deu para entender? ele já era baixo. Então o que que mudou foi simplesmente o Brasil está insustentável, as empresas não estão mais aguentando no regime fiscal brasileiro e estão buscando uma saída. E o Paraguai é o vizinho mais perto, é o vizinho mais fácil, é o vizinho do Mercosul, porque até a Argentina também é um manicômio tributário. Apesar do MEI tá lá cuidando de de algumas coisas, ele ainda não chegou na parte crucial que é baixar os impostos. Então sim, ainda tem um longo caminho pra Argentina também tem outros países ao redor, mas quem é o mais top da galáxia para empresas migrarem, principalmente brasileiras, é o Paraguai. E eu vou explicar nesse vídeo porquê. Primeiramente, o sistema tributário do Paraguai é simples. 10% de imposto sobre o lucro líquido. Sim, não é sobre o faturamento. Lucro líquido para a empresa, 10% de imposto para a pessoa física. Também dá para considerar que é lucro, mas é um pouco mais difícil de fazer as deduções, mas dá para fazer até da pessoa física dá para fazer. E 10% de imposto sobre consumo. Ou seja, se você comprar uma câmera, por exemplo, você vai pagar 10% de imposto só. Acabou. É o imposto triplo que eles fazem a pirâmide ali. Então, a base, você paga os impostos e cada cadeia gera crédito pra próxima. Vou explicar isso também. Quando você vai ter a sua empresa, você vai lá pagar seus impostos, correto? Você comprou vários insumos, mais ou menos assim, R$ 500.000 em insumos e 1 milhão de faturamento. Então você teve um lucro de R.000, porém não, você comprou coisas, certo? Você comprou R$ 500.000 em insumos e e gastos, etc. Tudo que você comprou, que foi pago o imposto de IVA daqueles 10% sobre o consumo, também gera crédito pro seu lucro. Então, naqueles 500.000, você vai ter alguns créditos tributários que vai incidir naquilo, benefícios fiscais também, que vai acabar diminuindo ainda mais. o seu lucro no papel, o lucro tributável. Então é muito benéfico, tipo, eles realmente te ajudam. É diferente do Brasil que não se importa se você lucrou ou não. Se você faturou, você paga. É a regra geral, OK? Só que assim, existe oportunidades ainda melhores, que é o regime de maquila. O regime de maquila envolve empresas que importam insumos, importam serviços ou conhecimento e daí manufatura, maquila, transforma dentro do Paraguai e exporta tudo aquilo que foi produzido ou pelo menos 90% da produção. Na prática, o seguinte, você trouxe ali várias barras de ferro, manufaturou aquilo, transformou aquilo em alguma peça e depois exportou aquelas peças. O valor agregado é aquilo que você agrega as peças. Ou seja, você gastou em torno de R$ 500.000 em insumos e agregou o valor e vendeu por 1 milhão no final ali na fatura. Só vai ser tributado 1% dos 500.000 a mais que você agregou o valor. Cara, é top, né? Top demais. E para quem ainda acha pouco, se você se enquadrar na lei 60 90, você também vai ter a isenção de imposto de importação e imposto de exportação. Ou seja, aquele material que você comprou, aqueles insumos, não custaram nada além do custo dele e o frete, não vai ter imposto em cima daquilo. E quando você jogar para fora e seu material, suas peças, também não vai ter imposto incidido pelo Paraguai, OK? Mas o Brasil vai cobrar imposto na importação daquele produto. Infelizmente não dá para escapar. Se você vai vender pro Brasil, você vai negociar com brasileiro, OK? Porém, você vai ter uma economia muito grande no custo operacional. Vai ser, vai ter uma economia muito grande também na mão de obra, folha salarial, energia e na importação. E daí você vai exportar e o que você tiver que pagar de tributo pro Brasil. Dá muitas vezes que foi feito esse cálculo, vale a pena, principalmente porque poxa, você economizou muito dinheiro, muito dinheiro mesmo. Vocês não tm noção quanto de grana dá para economizar seguindo a maquila e se o seu negócio se beneficiar disso, ok? Mas é só fábrica não. Você está atendendo empresas de fora. Isso se enquadra na maquila. trazer de fora um conhecimento, agregar valor no Paraguai, contratando mão de obra local, gastando, maquilando, né, transformando, gastando energia, local, né, os o aluguel do espaço e mandando para fora, ou seja, você atendendo estrangeiros. Nesse caso, é perfeito também, serviço também se enquadra. Então você pode fazer esse cálculo para consultorias, para serviços de marketing, para qualquer coisa que você consiga seguir esse padrão, importação, maquila, agregar valor e mandar para fora. Essas são as vantagens gigantescas que o Paraguai tem. Um regime tributário simplificado para quem trabalha de qualquer coisa, mas também regime especial para quem se enquadra nessa última situação que é maravilhosa.
